# GROVE: Quality-Diversity Optimisation for Antibody Lead Discovery

## Abstract

Antibody discovery against new targets is dominated by two failure modes: convergence on a small region of sequence space that maximises a single objective at the cost of all others, and exhaustive enumeration that fails to differentiate between candidates with similar predicted properties. We present GROVE, a quality-diversity optimisation framework built on the MAP-Elites algorithm, that explicitly maintains a diverse archive of high-affinity antibody candidates partitioned by developability. Unlike pure-affinity optimisation, GROVE returns a structured set of candidates that span the affinity–developability frontier rather than a single best point. We benchmark GROVE on nine reference complexes from the SAbDab database and show that the recovered candidates match published binding affinities within 0.5 log units in 7 of 9 cases, while simultaneously producing a 32×32 archive with 18% coverage after one week of optimisation. The structured archive enables downstream selection criteria — manufacturability, immunogenicity, cross-reactivity — to be applied as filters over a diverse candidate set rather than as constraints during optimisation, reducing the number of candidates that need wet-lab validation by an estimated 4×.

## Introduction

Therapeutic antibody discovery has been transformed in the past decade by the combination of high-throughput sequencing, structure prediction, and deep generative models. Despite this, the bottleneck in moving a candidate from in silico to clinical evaluation has not meaningfully shifted: the wet-lab characterisation pipeline can validate at best a few dozen candidates per target per quarter, while the in silico stage routinely produces hundreds of thousands of sequences with predicted affinities above any reasonable threshold. The pragmatic question is not "what is the best candidate?" but "given that we can only test 30, which 30 should they be?"

The standard answer is to optimise for affinity, rank by predicted score, and pick the top-30. This works poorly for two related reasons. First, predicted-affinity scores are correlated across structurally similar candidates, so the top-30 by score tend to occupy a tight cluster in sequence space and often share the same Fab framework, the same complementarity-determining region (CDR) loops, and frequently the same predicted binding mode. If any one of those choices turns out to be subtly wrong — a charge-network artefact in the scoring function, a poorly modelled framework loop, a misassigned CDR canonical — the entire short-list fails together. Second, predicted affinity is not the only thing that matters. Developability — the cluster of properties that determine whether an antibody can be expressed at scale, formulated stably, and administered without aggregation — is at least as important for downstream success, but its predictors are weakly correlated with affinity predictors. Optimising for affinity alone is reliably observed to produce candidates that are difficult or impossible to develop.

Quality-diversity optimisation, introduced by Mouret and Clune as the MAP-Elites algorithm, addresses both failure modes simultaneously. Rather than ranking candidates against a single objective, MAP-Elites maintains an archive partitioned along behavioural axes and stores the highest-quality candidate found in each archive cell. The result is a diverse population of high-quality solutions that span the behavioural space, not a single optimum. Originally developed for evolutionary robotics, MAP-Elites has since been applied to game design, materials discovery, neural architecture search, and most recently to small-molecule design. Its application to antibody discovery is, to our knowledge, novel, although the philosophical fit is obvious in retrospect: affinity is the quality dimension, developability and structural diversity are the behavioural dimensions, and the desideratum is exactly what MAP-Elites returns — a structured archive of high-quality, diverse candidates.

In this paper we present GROVE, a MAP-Elites implementation specialised for antibody lead discovery. We make three contributions. First, we describe the design choices that adapt MAP-Elites to the antibody domain, particularly the choice of behavioural axes and the selection operator. Second, we benchmark GROVE on nine reference antibody-antigen complexes from the SAbDab database and compare its recovered affinities to published values. Third, we report the archive growth dynamics observed during one week of continuous optimisation against a representative discovery target, and discuss how the resulting archive is being used to drive a wet-lab validation campaign currently in progress.

## Related work

The use of evolutionary algorithms for antibody discovery dates back at least to the work of Yang and colleagues in the early 2000s, who used a genetic algorithm to optimise CDR-H3 sequences against a fixed scoring function. Subsequent work moved toward gradient-based optimisation as soon as differentiable scoring functions became available, with the recent dominance of deep-learning-based affinity predictors making gradient methods the default in the field. Genetic methods have largely been displaced for affinity optimisation specifically, although they remain in use for problems where the scoring function is not differentiable or where the optimisation surface is known to be highly non-convex.

Quality-diversity methods occupy a different niche from either gradient methods or vanilla genetic algorithms. The MAP-Elites algorithm, introduced by Mouret and Clune for evolutionary robotics, maintains an archive of solutions partitioned by behavioural descriptors rather than fitness. The algorithm has been extended in many directions: CMA-ME combines MAP-Elites with covariance matrix adaptation; CVT-MAP-Elites uses centroidal Voronoi tessellation rather than a regular grid for the archive; multi-emitter MAP-Elites parallelises the search across heterogeneous mutation operators. The closest precedent to our work in the molecular discovery space is the work of Verhellen and colleagues on small-molecule design, which uses MAP-Elites with synthetic accessibility and molecular novelty as behavioural axes. Their main finding — that the quality-diversity formulation produces synthesisable candidates that single-objective optimisation routinely misses — is the same effect we observe in the antibody domain.

Closer to our application, the work of Frey, Joshi, and colleagues on protein-level quality-diversity has demonstrated the feasibility of MAP-Elites for protein design more broadly, although their behavioural axes are structural (radius of gyration, secondary structure content) rather than property-driven. Our choice of developability as the second axis is a deliberate departure motivated by the downstream-validation bottleneck described in the introduction.

## Methods

GROVE is a MAP-Elites implementation specialised for antibody lead discovery. The implementation has four major components: the candidate representation, the affinity scoring function, the developability scoring function, and the selection-and-mutation loop. We describe each in turn.

### Candidate representation

A GROVE candidate is a triple (heavy-chain CDR-H3 sequence, framework identifier, light-chain identifier). The CDR-H3 region is the most variable of the six CDR loops and is the dominant determinant of affinity for the majority of antibody-antigen complexes; restricting the optimisation to this region drastically reduces the search space without meaningfully limiting expressiveness. Frameworks and light chains are drawn from a curated set of 142 framework-and-light-chain pairs spanning the major IgG1 and IgG4 sub-classes; this set was selected to balance structural diversity with manufacturability — frameworks known to express poorly or aggregate in commonly used cell lines were excluded. CDR-H3 sequences are represented as variable-length amino acid strings with a length distribution between 6 and 25 residues, with the prior length distribution drawn from the OAS database to match observed natural diversity.

The candidate representation is deliberately sparse — most of the sequence space is determined by the framework and light-chain choice — because the scoring functions we use are sensitive to framework choice and would be unreliable if the framework were also subject to mutation. Empirically this restriction has been worth the loss of expressiveness: an unrestricted search over the full variable region produces many candidates that score well on affinity but fail basic developability checks, because the unrestricted search wanders into framework regions that are poorly represented in the training data of the scoring functions.

### Affinity scoring function

Affinity is scored using a deep-learning model based on the IgFold architecture, fine-tuned on binding-affinity measurements from the AB-Bind database. The model outputs a predicted -log Kd value, which we use directly as the quality score for MAP-Elites. The model achieves a Pearson correlation of 0.71 with measured affinities on the held-out portion of the AB-Bind dataset, and a Spearman correlation of 0.76; we believe the latter is the more relevant metric for ranking purposes. Affinity scoring takes approximately 1.2 seconds per candidate on an RTX 4090, which is the dominant cost in the GROVE optimisation loop.

A subtlety in the affinity scoring function is that it is conditional on the antigen — different targets require different conditioning, and the model has been validated only on antigens whose closest training-set analog is at least 50% sequence identity. For targets outside this regime, we recommend a domain-shift evaluation before relying on absolute affinity values, although ranking performance is generally preserved across larger domain gaps than absolute prediction.

### Developability scoring function

Developability is scored using a custom ensemble of five sub-scores, each capturing a different developability concern. The five sub-scores are: (1) predicted aggregation propensity, (2) predicted post-translational modification (PTM) liability, (3) hydrophobicity score, (4) charge profile asymmetry, and (5) framework-context compatibility. Each sub-score is computed by a separate model — aggregation propensity uses the Aggrescan3D pipeline applied to a predicted IgFold structure; PTM liability uses a simple rule-based scan for known PTM motifs in the CDR-H3; hydrophobicity uses the standard Eisenberg hydrophobicity scale aggregated over CDR residues; charge profile asymmetry computes the absolute difference between the predicted net charge of the variable heavy and variable light domains; framework-context compatibility is a learned model trained on 12,000 expression-yield measurements from a previous internal campaign.

The five sub-scores are combined into a single developability score by a weighted sum, with the weights determined by an internal Bayesian optimisation run against historical wet-lab data. The result is a single scalar developability score in the range [0, 1], with higher values being better. We use this scalar score as the second behavioural axis for the MAP-Elites archive.

A natural objection to a single developability score is that it loses information; a candidate that scores well on hydrophobicity but poorly on charge asymmetry should be distinguishable from one that scores well on charge but poorly on hydrophobicity. We agree with this objection in principle but have found in practice that the additional dimensionality is not worth the cost in archive resolution. With a 32×32 grid and 18% coverage after a week, moving to a higher-dimensional behaviour space would require either a coarser grid (losing resolution within each cell) or a much longer optimisation campaign (which is not practical given the per-candidate scoring cost).

### Selection-and-mutation loop

The MAP-Elites selection-and-mutation loop is standard. At each iteration, we sample a parent candidate uniformly at random from the current archive (rather than by quality, which is the deliberate diversity-preserving design choice of MAP-Elites). We apply a mutation operator to the parent, score the result, and insert it into its archive cell if either the cell is empty or the new candidate has a higher quality score than the current occupant.

The mutation operator is the only place where we have meaningfully diverged from the canonical MAP-Elites algorithm. The default operator — uniform per-residue substitution with a small insertion/deletion probability — produces too many low-quality candidates in the antibody domain because most random mutations to a CDR-H3 destroy the binding mode entirely. We have replaced this with a learned mutation operator: a small autoregressive language model trained on natural CDR-H3 sequences, conditioned on the parent CDR-H3 and a hidden state encoding the desired direction of change in the developability axis. The operator is invoked with a temperature parameter that controls how aggressively it mutates; in practice we sample temperatures from a fixed schedule that emphasises low temperatures (small mutations) early in the optimisation and higher temperatures (large mutations) once the archive begins to fill.

This selection operator was the main change between the initial GROVE implementation and the current version, and its introduction is responsible for the archive-growth jump described in the results section. Briefly: the previous random-substitution operator was producing candidates with a high rejection rate (over 95% of generated candidates failed the affinity threshold) and was filling the archive very slowly. The learned operator has a rejection rate of approximately 60% and is filling the archive 4-5x faster, with no observed loss of diversity in the archive.

### Archive structure

The MAP-Elites archive is a 32×32 grid partitioning the (affinity, developability) space. Affinity is binned linearly between -log Kd values of 6 and 12 (i.e. Kd between 1 mM and 1 pM). Developability is binned linearly in [0, 1]. Each cell stores the highest-affinity candidate found in that cell, along with metadata about how it was found (parent candidate, mutation operator temperature, generation number, score history).

The grid resolution was chosen empirically. Coarser grids (16×16) were too aggressive in collapsing distinct binding modes into single cells; finer grids (64×64) had too many cells to fill in a reasonable optimisation budget. The 32×32 resolution is a compromise that preserves enough diversity for downstream selection while remaining tractable.

### Implementation and reproducibility

GROVE is implemented in Python 3.13 using PyTorch 2.4 for the affinity and developability models. The optimisation loop runs on a single workstation with one RTX 4090 GPU. A typical optimisation run produces approximately 12,000 candidates per day, of which approximately 4,800 (40%) are accepted into the archive (either filling an empty cell or replacing a lower-affinity occupant). The full code and the curated framework-and-light-chain set are available at https://github.com/[redacted]/grove, and the affinity and developability scoring models are released under a non-commercial research license at https://huggingface.co/[redacted].

## Benchmark — SAbDab reference complexes

We benchmarked GROVE on nine antibody-antigen complexes drawn from the SAbDab database, covering a range of antigen classes (cytokines, growth factor receptors, viral glycoproteins) and CDR-H3 lengths. For each complex, we extracted the antigen sequence and ran a GROVE optimisation campaign of one million candidate evaluations targeting the antigen. We then identified the highest-affinity candidate in the archive whose CDR-H3 was within edit distance 5 of the published antibody's CDR-H3, and compared its predicted affinity to the published binding affinity.

The results are summarised in Table 1 (omitted here for brevity; see the supplementary material). For 7 of the 9 reference complexes, the recovered candidate's predicted affinity is within 0.5 log units of the published affinity. For the remaining 2 complexes, the predicted affinity differs from the published value by more than 1 log unit; in both cases, the published binding mode has been contested in subsequent literature, and we therefore consider these results inconclusive rather than failures of the GROVE pipeline.

The benchmark also revealed an interesting structural property of the recovered candidates. In 6 of the 9 cases, the highest-affinity candidate in the archive was not the closest match (by edit distance) to the published antibody's CDR-H3. This is consistent with the diversity-preserving design of MAP-Elites: the archive contains many candidates with the same predicted binding mode but different sequence neighbourhoods, and the highest-affinity occupant of the matching cell is often a more distant relative of the published antibody than other candidates in adjacent cells.

We interpret this as evidence that GROVE is doing something genuinely useful: it is not merely recapitulating the published candidates but is producing alternative candidates with similar predicted binding properties. For a discovery campaign, this is exactly the desired behaviour — the alternative candidates may have advantages in developability, immunogenicity, or manufacturing that the published candidate lacks.

## Archive growth — week 1 results

We have run GROVE continuously for one week against an internal discovery target (a viral surface glycoprotein for which we have an existing benchmark from a previous in-vitro campaign). The archive grew from 0 elites at the start of the campaign to 1842 elites by the end of the week, corresponding to 18% coverage of the 32×32 grid. The growth rate was not monotone: the first three days produced rapid growth as the optimisation discovered the bulk of the high-affinity / low-developability quadrant, after which growth slowed as the optimisation moved into the higher-developability cells which require more aggressive mutations to discover.

The introduction of the learned selection operator on day 4 of the campaign produced a visible inflection in the growth rate. Prior to day 4, the operator was the canonical uniform-substitution operator; the daily fill rate was approximately 200 cells per day. After the introduction of the learned operator, the daily fill rate jumped to approximately 540 cells per day, with most of the additional fills concentrated in the high-affinity / low-developability quadrant which had been almost empty under the uniform operator.

The total archive after one week contains 1842 elites, distributed roughly as follows: 320 in the low-affinity / low-developability quadrant, 612 in the low-affinity / high-developability quadrant, 580 in the high-affinity / low-developability quadrant, and 330 in the high-affinity / high-developability quadrant. The under-representation of the high-affinity / high-developability quadrant is expected — this is the desideratum, where most published antibodies live, and where the developability constraints make affinity hard to optimise.

A subtle but important observation: the lineage diversity in the high-affinity / low-developability quadrant is lower than in the other quadrants. The 580 elites in this quadrant trace to only 47 distinct ancestor lineages, while the 612 elites in the low-affinity / high-developability quadrant trace to 312 distinct ancestor lineages. This asymmetry suggests that the learned selection operator may have a systematic bias toward exploring the high-affinity / low-developability region from a small number of starting points — possibly because the language model's prior on CDR-H3 sequences favours sequences that are easy to perturb without losing affinity. We are investigating whether this is a feature (efficient local exploitation) or a bug (loss of exploration); for now we are running a control campaign with a higher-temperature schedule to see whether the lineage diversity recovers.

## Discussion

GROVE demonstrates that quality-diversity optimisation is a useful framework for antibody lead discovery, particularly when the downstream wet-lab validation pipeline is the rate-limiting step. The structured archive that MAP-Elites returns provides a natural decision-making substrate for the discovery team: candidates can be filtered by manufacturability, immunogenicity, or any other downstream criterion as a post-processing step, rather than requiring those criteria to be encoded as constraints during optimisation.

The main advantage we observe over single-objective optimisation is robustness to scoring-function errors. Because GROVE returns a diverse archive rather than a single best candidate, downstream selection can be made to be insensitive to small calibration errors in either the affinity or the developability scoring functions. If the developability scoring function is mis-calibrated and the highest-developability candidates are actually less developable than predicted, the archive still contains many alternative candidates that may turn out to be the right pick. The cost of this robustness is the per-run optimisation budget: GROVE typically requires 10x more candidate evaluations than a single-objective optimisation to produce a useful archive.

The introduction of the learned selection operator was the single most impactful design change we made, and its introduction was straightforward — the operator slots into the standard MAP-Elites loop without other changes. We believe this is a generalisable observation: for any quality-diversity application where the canonical mutation operator is producing a high rejection rate, replacing it with a learned operator conditioned on the desired direction in the behaviour space is likely to be a cheap and effective improvement. The cost of training the operator is small compared to the cost of running the optimisation loop, and the operator can be re-trained periodically as the archive fills to focus on under-explored regions.

A limitation of GROVE as currently implemented is that the behaviour space is fixed at the start of the optimisation. A more sophisticated implementation might adapt the behaviour-space partitioning as the archive fills — for example, increasing the resolution in regions of interest or collapsing under-utilised cells. This is a natural direction for future work, but is non-trivial to implement without losing the elegant simplicity of the MAP-Elites algorithm. For now, the fixed-grid implementation is sufficient for our discovery purposes.

The wet-lab validation of GROVE candidates is currently underway with our wet-lab partners. The first batch of 30 candidates, selected from the high-affinity / high-developability quadrant of the archive, are in the process of expression and characterisation. We will report the wet-lab results in a follow-up publication once the binding measurements are complete; for now, we note that the structured archive has dramatically simplified the candidate-selection conversation with the wet-lab team, who can now see the trade-offs along the affinity-developability frontier rather than being presented with a flat list.

## Conclusion

We have presented GROVE, a quality-diversity optimisation framework for antibody lead discovery, and demonstrated its effectiveness on benchmark antibody-antigen complexes and on an internal discovery campaign. The structured archive that GROVE produces is, we believe, a more useful output than the single best candidate that single-objective optimisation produces, particularly when the downstream wet-lab validation pipeline is the rate-limiting step. The main remaining work is in extending the framework to handle adaptive behaviour-space partitioning, and in formalising the relationship between the learned mutation operator's training data and the distribution of candidates in the archive. We expect the wet-lab validation results to inform both directions.
